worth_it_explanation: Impression discrepancy is the gap between what FreeWheel counted and what a downstream demand partner or the player counted for the same delivery. A gap that nobody measures can hide a billing dispute, a tracking failure, or a player that drops a meaningful share of impressions before they render. This check reads counts the publisher already has across FreeWheel and its partners, so it costs reconciliation time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a specific source of loss the publisher can address.
intro: Impression discrepancy is the difference between the impression count FreeWheel records as the ad server and the count a counterparty records for the same delivery, whether that counterparty is a downstream demand partner reconciling against its own logs or the video player and its measurement vendor counting on the rendered side. Some gap is expected because each side counts at a different point in the ad lifecycle, but a gap outside an agreed tolerance signals a billing risk or a tracking failure worth investigating. These checks confirm that the discrepancy is measured against a defined tolerance and that out-of-range gaps are traced to a cause rather than absorbed.

audit_checks.title: Impression discrepancy is not measured against a defined tolerance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: If the gap between FreeWheel's count and a counterparty's count is never measured against an agreed tolerance, a billing dispute or a tracking failure can persist unseen across many reporting periods. The counts that determine what a publisher invoices and what a partner pays drift apart with no trigger to investigate, so revenue tied to the difference goes uncollected or unexplained.

educate.base:
FreeWheel serves video and Connected TV (CTV) ads, and it records an impression count for every delivery it fulfills. A downstream demand partner that bought through FreeWheel records its own count for that same delivery, and the video player together with any third-party measurement vendor records a count on the rendered side. Those three numbers will rarely match to the unit because each side counts at a different point in the ad lifecycle and applies its own filtration.

The Media Rating Council (MRC) and IAB Tech Lab measurement guidelines draw the line that explains most of the gap. A 'served' impression is counted when the ad server releases the ad, while a rendered impression is counted later, once the ad begins to render in the player. The revised guidelines shifted valid impression counting to a 'count-on-begin-to-render' minimum, which sits later in the process than a served count. Any impression that is requested but never renders widens the gap between an ad-server served count and a rendered count, whether the player abandoned playback, the creative errored, or the user left.

A defined tolerance turns that expected gap into a measurable control. The publisher and the counterparty agree on an acceptable percentage difference, often stated as a single-digit percentage, and reconcile counts each period against that threshold. A gap inside the tolerance is treated as normal counting-point variance. A gap outside it triggers an investigation. Without a stated tolerance, every difference looks the same, and there is no point at which a drift becomes a flag.

A few signs point to discrepancy that is not measured against a tolerance:
  • No agreed tolerance percentage exists between FreeWheel and the downstream demand partners or the player-side measurement
  • Period-over-period reconciliation of FreeWheel counts against partner counts is not performed on a regular schedule
  • The publisher cannot state the current discrepancy percentage for its largest demand relationships
You may want to confirm that an agreed tolerance is in place for each significant counting relationship and that counts are reconciled against it on a set cadence. One approach is to pull FreeWheel's impression count and the counterparty's count for the same line items over a recent period, calculate the percentage gap, and compare it to the tolerance the contract or the measurement agreement specifies.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster running long-form video and CTV ad pods through FreeWheel, the player and device environment vary widely across smart TVs, set-top boxes, and mobile apps, and each environment fires impression tracking differently. You may want to reconcile discrepancy by device and app environment rather than on a blended number, so a CTV environment that drops impressions before render does not hide inside an aggregate that mostly reflects healthier web playback.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher running product and shoppable video with ads served through FreeWheel, the video often sits inside pages where playback starts on scroll or on a user action, so a meaningful share of served impressions may never reach render. You may want to set the tolerance with that abandonment pattern in mind and reconcile against the rendered-side count, so the gap is judged against what actually played rather than against everything requested.

learn_more.source_title: MRC, Joined by IAB Tech Lab and MMA, Announces Releases of Revised Ad Measurement Guidelines

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed reconciliation and reporting documentation for the ad-server console is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether a tolerance is agreed for each significant counting relationship and whether FreeWheel counts are reconciled against partner counts on a regular schedule.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Pull FreeWheel's impression count for the line items or deals tied to the largest demand relationships over a recent period.
Record whether a documented tolerance percentage exists for each of those relationships and where it is defined, for example a contract or a measurement agreement.
Note whether a period-over-period reconciliation against partner counts is run on a set cadence or whether discrepancy is checked only when someone raises a billing question.

partner_reporting.label: Demand partner or measurement reporting
steps: Obtain the counterparty's impression count for the same line items and period from the demand partner's reporting or the player-side measurement vendor.
Calculate the percentage gap between the FreeWheel count and the counterparty count for each relationship.
Compare each gap to the agreed tolerance, and record any relationship where no tolerance exists to compare against.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each significant counting relationship, whether an agreed tolerance exists, whether reconciliation runs on a regular cadence, and the current discrepancy percentage. A setup where a tolerance is defined and counts are reconciled against it on a schedule passes. A setup where no tolerance exists or where reconciliation is performed only in response to a dispute is a fail recorded with the relationships affected and the current gap where it can be calculated.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the tolerance, the reconciliation cadence, and the current discrepancy percentage for each counting relationship above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not measure its FreeWheel impression discrepancy against a defined tolerance for its significant counting relationships, so the gap between FreeWheel's count and a downstream partner or player-side count has no threshold that flags it for investigation. Some gap is expected because the ad server counts a served impression earlier than the player counts a rendered one, but without an agreed tolerance every difference looks the same and a billing risk or a tracking failure can persist unseen. {{voice_recommend}} agreeing a tolerance percentage with each significant counterparty, reconciling FreeWheel counts against partner and player-side counts on a set cadence, and treating any gap outside the tolerance as a trigger to investigate. After the tolerance is in place, recheck the largest relationships each period to confirm the discrepancy stays within range.

audit_checks.title: Out-of-range discrepancy is not traced to a source
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a discrepancy exceeds the tolerance and nobody traces it to a cause, the publisher cannot tell whether the gap is a player timeout, errored media, a tracking-pixel failure, or a difference in counting method. The loss keeps recurring because the underlying source is never identified, so the same revenue or reporting gap repeats period after period with no fix applied.

educate.base:
A tolerance tells a publisher when a discrepancy is large enough to matter. It does not tell the publisher why the gap opened. Tracing an out-of-range discrepancy to its source is the step that turns a flagged number into a fix, and in FreeWheel's video and Connected TV (CTV) context the likely sources fall into a small set of recurring patterns.

The first source is the counting point itself. The ad server counts a served impression when it releases the ad, while the player and any measurement vendor count later, once the ad begins to render. Every impression that is requested but never renders sits in the gap between the two counts, whether the user abandoned playback or skipped past the slot. A larger-than-usual share of abandoned playback shows up directly as a wider served-to-rendered discrepancy.

The second source is failed delivery inside the player. The Video Ad Serving Template (VAST) response carries the creative and the impression-tracking instructions, and the player is meant to fire the impression tracker at the agreed moment. A creative that errors, a media file that fails to load on a given device, or a tracking call that times out all suppress the rendered-side count while the served count stands. The third source is a genuine difference in method, where two systems define and filter an impression differently, including how each removes known invalid traffic before reporting.

A few sources are worth ruling in or out when a discrepancy runs out of range:
  • Player or device timeouts and abandoned playback, which inflate served counts relative to rendered counts
  • Errored creatives or media files that fail to render on specific players or device types
  • Impression-tracking calls that fail, fire late, or fire from an environment the counterparty filters
  • A difference in counting method or invalid-traffic filtration between FreeWheel and the counterparty
You may want to confirm that each out-of-range discrepancy is traced to one of these sources and resolved rather than absorbed. One approach is to break the discrepancy down by device, player, and creative to isolate where the gap concentrates. A gap that lives in one device type or one creative usually points to a delivery or tracking failure rather than to normal counting variance.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster, an out-of-range discrepancy on CTV inventory often traces to a specific device or app build where the player fires impression tracking unreliably or times out before render. You may want to break the gap down by device model and app version so the investigation lands on the environment causing the loss rather than treating the discrepancy as a property-wide problem.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher, an out-of-range discrepancy frequently traces to playback that starts on scroll or on a user action and is abandoned before the ad renders, which inflates the served count against the rendered one. You may want to separate the abandonment-driven gap from genuine tracking failures. The first is addressed by where and how the video is placed and the second by fixing the impression tracker.

learn_more.source_title: MRC, Joined by IAB Tech Lab and MMA, Announces Releases of Revised Ad Measurement Guidelines

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's per-device and per-creative delivery diagnostics for tracing discrepancy are in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether out-of-range discrepancies are broken down by device, player, and creative to find the source, and whether the identified source is resolved rather than absorbed.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Identify the line items or relationships where the current discrepancy exceeds the agreed tolerance.
Break each out-of-range discrepancy down by device type, player environment, and creative to see whether the gap concentrates in one segment.
Record where the gap concentrates, for example a single device type or one creative. Concentration points to a delivery or tracking failure rather than normal counting variance.

vast_inspection.label: VAST and tracker inspection
steps: For a sample of impressions in the segment carrying the gap, inspect the Video Ad Serving Template (VAST) response and confirm the impression-tracking call is present and well formed.
Check whether the creative or media file errors on the affected device or player, which would suppress the rendered count while the served count stands.

Note whether the source is a counting-point difference, abandoned playback, a failed or late tracking call, or a difference in counting method, and record the cause for each out-of-range relationship.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each out-of-range discrepancy, the segment where the gap concentrates and the source identified, for example abandoned playback, an errored creative, a failed tracking call, or a counting-method difference. A setup where every out-of-range gap is traced to a source and addressed passes. A setup where out-of-range gaps are recorded but never traced, or are absorbed without investigation, is a fail recorded with the relationships and the gap left unexplained.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the segment and the identified source for each out-of-range discrepancy above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} flags discrepancies that exceed its tolerance but does not trace them to a source, so the publisher cannot tell whether a given gap is a player timeout, an errored creative, a failed tracking call, or a difference in counting method. Without the source, the same loss recurs because the underlying cause is never fixed. {{voice_recommend}} breaking each out-of-range discrepancy down by device, player, and creative to isolate where the gap concentrates, inspecting the Video Ad Serving Template (VAST) response and the impression tracker for the affected segment, and resolving the identified cause. After tracing the source, recheck the affected relationship the following period to confirm the gap returns within tolerance.
